tips.title: Medical Director Agreements Need Annual Review
tips.body: Your medical director agreement should be reviewed every year. Not just when something goes wrong. Make sure it specifies the physician's duties, availability requirements, the procedures they supervise, and how they'll be compensated. A vague or outdated agreement is one of the top reasons medspas lose their licenses in state audits.

tips.title: Track Every Provider's License Expiration in a Single Spreadsheet
tips.body: Keep a single spreadsheet listing every provider's license type, number, expiration date, and renewal lead time. Set calendar reminders 90 days before each expiration. One lapsed license discovered during a state inspection can shut down your entire operation until the paperwork is resolved, even if the provider is still qualified.

tips.title: Document Your Adverse Event Protocol
tips.body: Every medspa should have a written adverse event protocol: what to do if a client has a reaction, who to call, how to document it, and when to refer to emergency care. Post it in every treatment room. Regulators look for this during inspections, and having it demonstrates you take patient safety seriously.

tips.title: Post All Required Licenses Where Clients Can See Them
tips.body: Most states require you to post your salon license, each operator's license, and your health department permit in a visible location, usually near the front desk or in each service area. Not posting these can result in immediate fines during inspections. Do a 'compliance walk' once a month to confirm everything is current and posted.

tips.title: Booth Rental vs. Employee: Know the Difference Before the IRS Does
tips.body: If you control when a stylist works, what they charge, or what products they use, they're probably an employee, not an independent contractor. Misclassification can cost you years of back payroll taxes, penalties, and interest. Review each independent contractor relationship against the IRS common law test at least annually.

tips.title: Disinfectant Logs Protect You When Inspectors Come
tips.body: Keep a written or digital log of every time you disinfect tools, surfaces, and footbaths. Note the product used, concentration, contact time, and who performed the task. Inspectors frequently ask for these logs, and a business with six months of clean records is treated very differently than one with nothing to show.

tips.title: Your Liability Waiver Is Only As Good As Its Language
tips.body: A liability waiver that's buried in your membership agreement or uses outdated language may not hold up in court. Each state has different enforceability standards. Some require specific wording about 'inherent risks,' others require standalone documents. Have an attorney review your waiver when you open, and after any major state legislation changes.

tips.title: AED Inspections Should Be on Your Monthly Checklist
tips.body: Your AED (automated external defibrillator) needs monthly visual inspections and annual professional service. Inspect the indicator light (green = ready), check the battery expiration, confirm the pads are sealed and not expired, and log every check. In a cardiac emergency, a non-functional AED creates both a tragedy and a lawsuit.

tips.title: Personal Trainers as Independent Contractors: Know Your Risk
tips.body: Classifying personal trainers as independent contractors is common, but it comes with real compliance risk. Trainers who work exclusively at your facility, use your equipment, and follow your scheduling are usually employees under most state definitions. Misclassification exposes you to workers' comp liability if they're injured on the job.

tips.title: Before/After Photos Need a Results Disclosure
tips.body: The FTC requires that before/after photos in health and wellness advertising include a disclosure when results are not typical. Something like 'Results may vary. Individual results are not guaranteed' is a minimum, but the more specific you are about what typical results look like, the safer you are from enforcement action.

tips.title: Don't Advertise Services You're Not Licensed to Perform
tips.body: Some salon services, like certain chemical treatments, scalp massages billed as 'therapeutic,' or skin care services, cross the line into cosmetology, esthetics, or even nursing scope of practice depending on your state. Advertising a service you aren't licensed for can trigger a complaint with your licensing board, even if you haven't performed it yet.

tips.title: Review Your General Liability Coverage Annually
tips.body: Your gym's general liability insurance should be reviewed every year as your business grows. If you've added classes, new equipment, a smoothie bar, or personal training services since your last policy update, your current coverage may have gaps. Get a certificate of insurance and compare your coverage against your actual services at each renewal.

tips.title: Keep Copies of Every Inspection Report
tips.body: After every health department, licensing board, or fire marshal inspection, make a copy of the report and keep it in a dedicated compliance folder, physical and digital. Future inspectors often ask to see prior reports, and having them organized shows you're proactive. Also: address every deficiency in writing, even minor ones.

tips.title: Post All Required Federal and State Labor Law Notices
tips.body: Federal law requires you to post notices from OSHA, the DOL (FLSA, FMLA if applicable), and the EEOC. Most states add their own required posters. These must be physically posted where employees can see them, not just in the employee handbook. You can download free posters from the DOL website. Update them when laws change.
